categories.title: Basic static SVG controls
categories.description: Baseline primitives and non-animated SVG features.

categories.title: SMIL animation primitives
categories.description: SVG-native animation elements such as animate and animateTransform.

categories.title: Stroke drawing effects
categories.description: Dash-based reveal and looping stroke animations.

categories.title: CSS-based SVG animation experiments
categories.description: Inline style blocks, classes, and CSS keyframe animation inside SVG.

categories.title: Feature edge cases
categories.description: Reuse, filters, and features that are often sanitized or inconsistent.

examples.title: clipPath example
examples.description: A clipped pattern inside a circle to verify clipPath defs usage.
examples.compatibilityNotes: clipPath often works, but it is useful to verify defs-heavy features directly in GitHub.

examples.title: Gradient fill
examples.description: Linear gradient with a simple glossy badge shape.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Gradient defs are commonly supported, but they verify that defs survive the renderer.

examples.title: Path and stroke
examples.description: Curved path with visible control-point markers and thick stroke styling.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Complex paths still render as plain SVG and are usually safe on GitHub.

examples.title: Simple circle
examples.description: Single filled circle with a baseline label and no animation.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Plain shape rendering is typically stable in browsers and GitHub image rendering.

examples.title: Simple rect
examples.description: Rounded rectangle with stroke and layered highlight.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Inline rectangle geometry and stroke attributes should be broadly compatible.

examples.title: Text example
examples.description: Mixed text sizes and weights to probe plain SVG text rendering.
examples.compatibilityNotes: SVG text generally works, though font metrics may vary between environments.

examples.title: SMIL additive accumulate
examples.description: Stacks repeated translation and rotation using additive and accumulate behavior.
examples.compatibilityNotes: More advanced SMIL timing model coverage; if unsupported, the shape may appear static or partially animated.

examples.title: SMIL animate for cx
examples.description: Moves a circle horizontally by animating the cx attribute.
examples.compatibilityNotes: A direct animate-on-attribute test. Browsers usually support it, while GitHub may show only the initial frame.

examples.title: SMIL animate for fill
examples.description: Cycles a rectangle through multiple fill colors.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Good for checking whether animated presentation attributes survive rendering.

examples.title: SMIL animateMotion path follow
examples.description: Moves a dot along a visible guide path.
examples.compatibilityNotes: animateMotion is historically less universal than basic animate, making it a good compatibility probe.

examples.title: SMIL multiple animate elements
examples.description: Animates position, radius, and fill on one element at the same time.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Good for testing whether multiple child animation nodes on the same target are all honored.

examples.title: SMIL opacity pulse
examples.description: Pulses element opacity with a simple animate element.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Useful for determining whether opacity changes animate or only the static state is shown.

examples.title: SMIL set with timing offsets
examples.description: Uses set elements to toggle visibility with begin offsets and durations.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Tests set timing behavior, which can be easier to reason about than full interpolation.

examples.title: SMIL transform rotate, scale, translate
examples.description: Runs three animateTransform variants side-by-side.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Combines rotate, scale, and translate to compare transform-specific behavior in one SVG.

examples.title: Circular progress animation
examples.description: A circular arc animates with stroke dashes like a progress ring.
examples.compatibilityNotes: The static ring should render everywhere; the animated progress effect may be browser-only in practice.

examples.title: Stroke dash reveal
examples.description: Uses dash offset animation to reveal a polyline drawing.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Dash styling usually renders on GitHub, but animation may not play there.

examples.title: Looping line draw
examples.description: A looping rectangular route continuously redraws itself.
examples.compatibilityNotes: This is still SMIL-based, so browser support is stronger than GitHub playback.

examples.title: CSS color transition
examples.description: Class-based color cycling for a central badge.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Embedded style tags may be preserved in some contexts but are not guaranteed on GitHub.

examples.title: CSS keyframes movement
examples.description: Inline style block animating horizontal movement with keyframes.
examples.compatibilityNotes: CSS animation inside SVG tends to work in browsers, but GitHub may render a static first frame.

examples.title: CSS opacity blink
examples.description: Simple blinking dots using a shared class and staggered timing.
examples.compatibilityNotes: A useful experiment for whether CSS animation survives sanitization or becomes a static snapshot.

examples.title: CSS stroke reveal
examples.description: Path reveal using stroke-dashoffset animated through CSS.
examples.compatibilityNotes: This combines class-based styling and dash animation, which is informative for browser-vs-GitHub comparisons.

examples.title: Filter with drop shadow
examples.description: Uses a blur and offset filter chain to simulate a shadowed card.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Basic filters often work in browsers, but GitHub may sanitize or flatten them differently.

examples.title: foreignObject HTML block
examples.description: Embeds HTML inside the SVG as a browser-oriented compatibility edge case.
examples.expectation: Likely sanitized or ignored on GitHub
examples.compatibilityNotes: foreignObject content is a known sanitization risk and is often stripped or rendered inconsistently on GitHub.

examples.title: Script tag negative test
examples.description: Includes a tiny script to confirm that active content is blocked or ignored.
examples.expectation: Likely sanitized or ignored on GitHub
examples.compatibilityNotes: Script execution in SVG is typically blocked in repository rendering for security reasons.

examples.title: Symbol and use reuse
examples.description: Reuses one symbol instance at multiple sizes to test defs-based reuse.
examples.compatibilityNotes: Symbol and use are usually preserved, but symbol references are still worth checking in repository rendering.
